episodes.title: Ashfall
episodes.logline: A teenage scavenger looking for a spare power cell in a dead colony instead wakes the most wanted war machine in the Tiers.

styles.label: Pure Cut
styles.blurb: The scene, raw. Voices, comms, mecha, and ambient sound only — like watching with your eyes closed. Most immersive, asks the most of your imagination.

cues.name: Kestrel
cues.text: Come on. Come on. There has to be one clean power cell in this whole graveyard.

cues.name: Kestrel
cues.text: That is not the structure settling.

cues.name: Compact PA
cues.text: Attention. This sector is under Meridian Compact jurisdiction. Unauthorized salvage will be met with lethal force. You have thirty seconds to comply.

cues.name: Kestrel
cues.text: Thirty seconds. Great. Generous.

cues.name: Kestrel
cues.text: The old fabrication bay. They never stripped the fabrication bay.

cues.name: Kestrel
cues.text: ...Okay. That is definitely not a power cell.

cues.name: Echo
cues.text: Cradle integrity nominal. Reactor at four percent, and climbing. ... Pilot detected.

cues.name: Kestrel
cues.text: Nope. Nope, nope, nope. I am a scavenger. I scavenge. I do not pilot the giant murder machine.

cues.name: Echo
cues.text: You are the only living operator in range. Designation of this unit: Exile. I am its intelligence. You may call me Echo.

cues.name: Kestrel
cues.text: I may call you a really bad idea.

cues.name: Echo
cues.text: Noted. The Compact will breach this bay in ninety seconds. Statistically, the murder machine improves your outcome.

cues.name: Rask
cues.text: Compact lance, this is Rask. Heat signature in the fab bay just spiked. Something down there woke up. ... Burn it.

cues.name: Kestrel
cues.text: Who is Rask?

cues.name: Echo
cues.text: The man who is about to kill you. Cradle release?

cues.name: Kestrel
cues.text: ...Do it. Do it, do it, do it.

cues.name: Echo
cues.text: Neural sync at sixty percent. Try not to think too hard. It listens.

cues.name: Kestrel
cues.text: It listens to — whoa. Whoa, I felt that. I felt the arm.

cues.name: Echo
cues.text: Incoming. Rask's frame. The Harrow. He is the Compact's best. Please understand what that means.

cues.name: Rask
cues.text: A child. They woke the Exile for a child. ... This is almost an insult.

cues.name: Kestrel
cues.text: He is fast — Echo, he is too fast—

cues.name: Echo
cues.text: Then stop aiming. Stop thinking like a gun. Move like you are running. You are good at running.

cues.name: Kestrel
cues.text: ...Yeah. Yeah, okay. Running I can do.

cues.name: Rask
cues.text: ...Interesting.

cues.name: Rask
cues.text: All units, pull back. Mark the frame. We do not lose the Exile twice.

cues.name: Kestrel
cues.text: ...Did we just—

cues.name: Echo
cues.text: Survive? Barely. You are bleeding from a console you headbutted.

cues.name: Kestrel
cues.text: Worth it.

cues.name: Vale
cues.text: Unregistered Exile-class frame. I have waited twelve years to hear that reactor sing again. ... I did not expect a kid at the helm.

cues.name: Kestrel
cues.text: Who is this?

cues.name: Vale
cues.text: My name is Vale. I lead what is left of the people the Compact tried to erase. And you, son, have just stolen the single most wanted machine in the Tiers.

cues.name: Kestrel
cues.text: ...I really just wanted a power cell.

cues.name: Vale
cues.text: They always do. Bring it home, Kestrel. We have a great deal to talk about.

styles.label: Audio-Described
styles.tag: A narrator paints the action between the lines.
styles.blurb: Every visual beat is described by a narrator, the way audio description works for film. You will never lose the plot — ideal for a long ride where you can't glance at a screen.

cues.name: Narrator
cues.text: A dead place. Wind moves through the gutted ribs of Colony Ring Nine, abandoned twelve years. In the dark, a boy with a wrist lamp picks through a drift of scrap. His name is Kestrel. He is seventeen, and he is not supposed to be here.

cues.name: Kestrel
cues.text: Come on. Come on. There has to be one clean power cell in this whole graveyard.

cues.name: Narrator
cues.text: Far off, a sound that does not belong to a dead colony. A low thud. Then another, closer.

cues.name: Kestrel
cues.text: That is not the structure settling.

cues.name: Compact PA
cues.text: Attention. This sector is under Meridian Compact jurisdiction. Unauthorized salvage will be met with lethal force. You have thirty seconds to comply.

cues.name: Kestrel
cues.text: Thirty seconds. Great. Generous.

cues.name: Narrator
cues.text: Kestrel runs. Behind him the corridor lights stutter awake, red, one by one, as Compact drones flood the ring.

cues.name: Kestrel
cues.text: The old fabrication bay. They never stripped the fabrication bay.

cues.name: Narrator
cues.text: He shoulders through a half-sealed blast door into a cavern of black air. His lamp barely reaches the far wall. And then it catches something. A shape. Forty meters of dormant steel hung in a cradle of dead gantries. Humanoid. Sleeping. Waiting.

cues.name: Kestrel
cues.text: ...Okay. That is definitely not a power cell.

cues.name: Narrator
cues.text: As he steps closer, something deep in the machine answers.

cues.name: Echo
cues.text: Cradle integrity nominal. Reactor at four percent, and climbing. ... Pilot detected.

cues.name: Kestrel
cues.text: Nope. Nope, nope, nope. I am a scavenger. I scavenge. I do not pilot the giant murder machine.

cues.name: Echo
cues.text: You are the only living operator in range. Designation of this unit: Exile. I am its intelligence. You may call me Echo.

cues.name: Kestrel
cues.text: I may call you a really bad idea.

cues.name: Echo
cues.text: Noted. The Compact will breach this bay in ninety seconds. Statistically, the murder machine improves your outcome.

cues.name: Rask
cues.text: Compact lance, this is Rask. Heat signature in the fab bay just spiked. Something down there woke up. ... Burn it.

cues.name: Kestrel
cues.text: Who is Rask?

cues.name: Echo
cues.text: The man who is about to kill you. Cradle release?

cues.name: Kestrel
cues.text: ...Do it. Do it, do it, do it.

cues.name: Narrator
cues.text: The cradle clamps blow. Forty meters of forgotten war drops, catches itself on screaming thrusters, and stands — for the first time in twelve years — with a terrified teenager wired into its spine.

cues.name: Echo
cues.text: Neural sync at sixty percent. Try not to think too hard. It listens.

cues.name: Kestrel
cues.text: It listens to — whoa. Whoa, I felt that. I felt the arm.

cues.name: Echo
cues.text: Incoming. Rask's frame. The Harrow. He is the Compact's best. Please understand what that means.

cues.name: Rask
cues.text: A child. They woke the Exile for a child. ... This is almost an insult.

cues.name: Narrator
cues.text: The Harrow comes out of the dark like a thrown knife.

cues.name: Kestrel
cues.text: He is fast — Echo, he is too fast—

cues.name: Echo
cues.text: Then stop aiming. Stop thinking like a gun. Move like you are running. You are good at running.

cues.name: Kestrel
cues.text: ...Yeah. Yeah, okay. Running I can do.

cues.name: Narrator
cues.text: And the Exile moves — not like a soldier, but like a kid who has spent his whole life slipping through gaps that were never meant for him. It ducks under the Harrow's blade and lets the colony's broken bones do the rest.

cues.name: Rask
cues.text: ...Interesting.

cues.name: Rask
cues.text: All units, pull back. Mark the frame. We do not lose the Exile twice.

cues.name: Narrator
cues.text: They break through the colony's shattered outer ring into open dark. Earth hangs below, a bruised blue marble. And for one second there is only the sound of Kestrel breathing.

cues.name: Kestrel
cues.text: ...Did we just—

cues.name: Echo
cues.text: Survive? Barely. You are bleeding from a console you headbutted.

cues.name: Kestrel
cues.text: Worth it.

cues.name: Vale
cues.text: Unregistered Exile-class frame. I have waited twelve years to hear that reactor sing again. ... I did not expect a kid at the helm.

cues.name: Kestrel
cues.text: Who is this?

cues.name: Vale
cues.text: My name is Vale. I lead what is left of the people the Compact tried to erase. And you, son, have just stolen the single most wanted machine in the Tiers.

cues.name: Kestrel
cues.text: ...I really just wanted a power cell.

cues.name: Vale
cues.text: They always do. Bring it home, Kestrel. We have a great deal to talk about.

cues.name: Narrator
cues.text: Above a dying world, a stolen legend turns toward the dark — and the boy inside it has no idea he has just become the most hunted person alive.

styles.label: Recap Hosts
styles.tag: Two hosts react and riff, with clips dropped in.
styles.blurb: A podcast ABOUT the episode — two hosts recap, joke, and pull in real clips. Lightest and most fun, least faithful. Great for catching up without committing.

cues.name: Delray
cues.text: Welcome back to Frame Theory, the show where we watch giant robots make terrible decisions so you do not have to. I am Delray.

cues.name: Pip
cues.text: And I am Pip, and this week — oh, this week. We are cracking open Mobile Frame: Exile, episode one, Ashfall. Delray. They gave the forty-meter doomsday weapon to a teenager.

cues.name: Delray
cues.text: They gave it to a scavenger, Pip. A kid who is, and I quote—

cues.name: Kestrel
cues.text: ...Okay. That is definitely not a power cell.

cues.name: Pip
cues.text: That is not a power cell! He is just trying to find a battery in a dead colony, and he trips over a war crime in a closet.

cues.name: Delray
cues.text: And can we talk about Echo? Because the ship A.I. has the driest delivery I have heard in years. The colony is about to be breached, the kid is panicking, and Echo just goes—

cues.name: Echo
cues.text: Noted. The Compact will breach this bay in ninety seconds. Statistically, the murder machine improves your outcome.

cues.name: Pip
cues.text: Statistically. I am obsessed. That is my new coworker.

cues.name: Delray
cues.text: Now here is what actually got me. The fight. Rask — the Compact's best pilot — he expects a soldier. And instead the show does this really smart thing where the kid's only real skill is running away.

cues.name: Pip
cues.text: Right, and the A.I. leans into it. It is not become a warrior, it is—

cues.name: Kestrel
cues.text: ...Yeah. Yeah, okay. Running I can do.

cues.name: Delray
cues.text: It is a fight scene built entirely around the protagonist's one talent, which is cowardice with excellent cardio.

cues.name: Pip
cues.text: And then the gut punch. Vale shows up, tells him he just stole the most wanted machine in the Tiers, and the kid just—

cues.name: Kestrel
cues.text: ...I really just wanted a power cell.

cues.name: Delray
cues.text: Best last line of any premiere this season. Episode one, Ashfall. We are so in.

cues.name: Pip
cues.text: Next week: apparently the murder machine has opinions. We will see you then.

episodes.title: The Real Robot Revolution
episodes.logline: The hosts go back to 1979's Mobile Suit Gundam — the show that turned giant robots from superheroes into equipment, and rewrote the genre forever.

styles.label: Recap Hosts
styles.tag: Two hosts dig into a classic — pure commentary.
styles.blurb: Delray and Pip break down a landmark series in their own words: history, craft, hot takes, and why it still matters. Transformative commentary — no clips, just analysis.

cues.name: Delray
cues.text: Welcome back to Frame Theory. I am Delray, and today we are not cracking open a new show — we are going back to the one that started literally all of this. Nineteen seventy-nine. Mobile Suit Gundam.

cues.name: Pip
cues.text: I am Pip, and here is the thesis, right up front: this is the show that split the entire giant-robot world in half. Before it, robots were superheroes. After it, robots were equipment.

cues.name: Delray
cues.text: That is the whole revolution in one word. Equipment. The mobile suits are mass-produced military hardware. They run out of ammo. They need maintenance. They get assigned to scared teenagers who were drafted into a war they did not start.

cues.name: Pip
cues.text: Amuro is not a chosen one. He is a civilian kid who climbs into the prototype because the trained adults around him are already dead and somebody has to pull the lever.

cues.name: Delray
cues.text: And then there is the rival. The masked antagonist basically invented an archetype that the next forty-five years of anime are still paying rent on. The tragic grudge, the custom color-coded unit, the soldier who is too charismatic to be a villain and too bitter to be a hero.

cues.name: Pip
cues.text: They built a calendar, Delray. A whole fake history — the Universal Century — so internally consistent that grown adults now write essays about the economics of space colonies.

cues.name: Delray
cues.text: And underneath the war story there is this quiet, almost spiritual idea — that living out in space might be slowly evolving human empathy into something close to psychic. The show is really asking whether war is just a failure of people to understand each other.

cues.name: Pip
cues.text: Does it all hold up? Honestly, no. The pacing is nineteen seventy-nine television pacing. There is a monster-of-the-week skeleton bolted onto an anti-war epic, and you can feel the bolts.

cues.name: Delray
cues.text: But when it lands — the moment a child soldier realizes the pilot he just shot down was a person with a name and a family — that single beat is the genetic code of every serious mecha show since. Macross. Evangelion. The original drama we run on this very feed.

cues.name: Pip
cues.text: Ride recommendation: do not binge it like a modern series. Let it breathe. One or two a day, like reading a war diary.

cues.name: Delray
cues.text: That is the real robot revolution. Robots stopped being gods and started being machines that people have to live and die inside. Next time, we go loud. Very loud.

styles.label: Audio Primer
styles.blurb: A short, calm primer that sets up the series before you dive in — what it is, why it matters, what to listen for. Perfect for the first few minutes of a ride.

cues.name: Narrator
cues.text: A Frame Theory primer, for the road. No spoilers that matter — just enough to ride in knowing why this one is a pillar.

cues.name: Narrator
cues.text: The original Mobile Suit Gundam is the founding text of what fans call the real robot genre. The giant machines are not magic. They are tanks with arms — mass-produced, fuel-hungry, and only as good as the frightened young pilots strapped inside them.

cues.name: Narrator
cues.text: The story follows a teenage civilian thrown behind the controls of an experimental unit when his colony is caught in a war between Earth and its breakaway space settlements.

cues.name: Narrator
cues.text: Its lasting ideas are simple and heavy. War is logistics and grief, not glory. The enemy is a person. And a new kind of human, sharpened by life in space, might just be able to feel that across a battlefield.

cues.name: Narrator
cues.text: If you only know the genre through its descendants, this is the source of the river. Watch for the masked rival; he is the blueprint for nearly every anime antagonist you already love.

cues.name: Narrator
cues.text: That is your primer. Now go ride in knowing where the road began.

episodes.title: Power Levels & Patience
episodes.logline: The loudest show in the genre, taken apart with love: why Dragon Ball Z's treadmill of escalation works, and why it's secretly the perfect thing to listen to.

styles.label: Recap Hosts
styles.tag: Two hosts dig into a classic — pure commentary.
styles.blurb: Delray and Pip break down a landmark series in their own words: history, craft, hot takes, and why it still matters. Transformative commentary — no clips, just analysis.

cues.name: Delray
cues.text: Frame Theory is back. I am Delray, and after a week in the quiet grief of classic Gundam, we are doing the loudest show in the entire genre. Dragon Ball Z.

cues.name: Pip
cues.text: Pip here, and let me give you the elevator pitch the way an honest person would: this is the show that taught a whole generation that any problem can be solved by screaming until your hair changes color.

cues.name: Delray
cues.text: And structurally it is brilliant and infuriating at the exact same time. It is a treadmill of escalation. Every arc runs the same loop: a new threat stronger than the last one, the heroes train, they surpass it, repeat forever.

cues.name: Pip
cues.text: But here is the part people get wrong. The engine of this show is not the fighting. It is the waiting. The charge-up. They monetized anticipation. A single punch can take three episodes to actually land.

cues.name: Delray
cues.text: And somehow it works, because the real stakes are emotional, not tactical. The lead is not fighting to save the universe. He is fighting because he wants a stronger opponent. He is a cheerful zen monk with the temperament of a golden retriever.

cues.name: Pip
cues.text: Meanwhile the prince — the proud rival — is the best-written character in the whole thing and everyone quietly knows it. Pride as a tragic flaw. A redemption arc you can measure in how many years it takes him to admit that he cares about anyone.

cues.name: Delray
cues.text: We have to mention the power-level discourse. The show introduces these precise numeric strength readings, and then abandons them almost immediately, because numbers physically cannot keep up with that much escalation. After a point it is running on pure vibes.

cues.name: Pip
cues.text: Fair criticisms: the filler, the recaps, and the way the deep, capable female cast gets benched the second the real punching starts.

cues.name: Delray
cues.text: But the craft of a climax? Dragon Ball Z might be the best in all of anime at the long, earned, hair-raising peak. The build is endless, but the payoff genuinely lifts you out of your seat.

cues.name: Pip
cues.text: Which makes it the perfect audio commute show, weirdly. You do not need a screen to know the hero is powering up. You can hear it. The whole soundtrack is anticipation.

cues.name: Delray
cues.text: Power levels and patience. That is the entire machine. Ride safe, and we will see you next time on Frame Theory.

styles.label: Audio Primer
styles.blurb: A short, calm primer that sets up the series before you dive in — what it is, why it matters, what to listen for. Perfect for the first few minutes of a ride.

cues.name: Narrator
cues.text: A Frame Theory primer, for the road. Spoiler-light — just the shape of the thing, so you can ride in knowing what you are hearing.

cues.name: Narrator
cues.text: Dragon Ball Z is the loudest, most influential action anime ever made. It follows a circle of martial artists who defend their world from a rising ladder of ever-stronger threats — aliens, androids, and worse.

cues.name: Narrator
cues.text: Its rhythm is unique. Battles are enormous, drawn-out events built around the charge — the slow, deliberate gathering of power before release. The anticipation is the point, and the sound design carries it.

cues.name: Narrator
cues.text: Underneath the spectacle is a simple emotional core: a hero who fights not for glory but for the joy of a worthy opponent, and a rival whose entire arc is learning, very slowly, how to care.

cues.name: Narrator
cues.text: Because so much of its drama lives in voice, music, and the rising hum before a clash, it travels beautifully without a screen. You will always know when the moment is coming.

cues.name: Narrator
cues.text: That is your primer. Eyes on the road, ears on the charge.
